text:
More than 10,000 athletes from 206 countries have now convened in Paris, where this Friday the flame will be lit starting the 2024 summer Olympic Games.

The countries of the Balkans, like all others, pin their hopes on their athletes coming back with at least some medals around their necks.

With 102 athletes,Turkeyis eyeing medals in several fields, such as boxing, wrestling, archery and taekwondo. But the country’s biggest hope of a gold is the women’s volleyball team, also known as “Sultans of the Net”.

“The women’s volleyball team is going to Paris to win the country’s most symbolic medal,” Alp Ulagay, a Turkish sportswriter, told BIRN. He added that under the captaincy of veteran Eda Erdem, the Sultans will try to win the country’s first-ever medal in team sports.

“Turkey is not a very important Olympic country… and we do not have one of the largest Olympic teams, but Turkey still has some high hopes for women’s boxing, archery and taekwondo,” Ulagay said.

Players of Turkey celebrate winning the EuroVolley Women 2023 gold medal match volleyball match between Serbia and Turkey, in Brussels, Belgium, 03 September 2023. EPA-EFE/OLIVIER MATTHYS

InCroatia, there are hopes that the Paris games will see yet more medals going to rower brothers Martin and Valent Sinkovic who already have two Olympic golds, won in Rio and Tokyo, and a silver won in London.

Valent,35, and Martin, 36, are the country’s biggest hope of winning a gold in Paris.

Dragutin Kerze, a sports journalist with more than 40 years of experience, said: “The Sinkovic brothers are certainly the biggest hope for a gold medal but we shouldn’t forget Matea Jelic, a taekwondo fighter who is also a gold medalist from Tokyo, and Nikola Mektic and Mate Pavic, a gold tennis pair from Tokyo.”

With only five athletes in Paris,Bosnia and Herzegovinawill compete in bowl throwing, judo and swimming. Among the five representing the country is 18-year-old swimmer Lana Pudar, who already won junior European and World Championships in the 100 and 200-metre categories.

Sinan Sinanovic, editor of online sports media outlet Sportske.ba, sees Pudar as a potential contender for medals. “Pudar is one of the most successful sportswomen in Bosnian history and I’m certain that she will impress the entire region,” he told BIRN.

Sinanovic said Pudar could even help change the public perspective on sports in Bosnia, where the major interest remains football. “Besides being a favourite, I’m sure that everyone will be cheering for her,” he added.

Kosovo, which will have nine athletes at the Olympic Games, and judo is the biggest hope of a medal. The country’s first-ever Olympic games was Rio in 2016, where Majlinda Kelmendi took a gold.

“Judo remains the biggest potential for medals and I think Kosovo will win at least one of the three first places,” Driton Latifi, a Kosovo sports journalist, told BIRN. “Distria Krasniqi is my favorite to win a medal this time,” he added.

“Other athletes in swimming and athletics will have more challenges and will try to improve their results,” Latifi added.

Greece’sOlympic team, with 101 athletes, is expecting success in the sport of rings, men’s water polo and others.

“I am optimistic that, apart from winning medals, Greek athletes will produce standout performances,” Panagiotis Loutas, sports journalist at TV COSMOTE, said.

“Miltos Tentoglou, 26, a long-jump athlete and Olympic gold medalist, stands out due to his recent achievements,” he added.

Tentoglou won his third straight European outdoor title at the European Athletics Championships in Rome in June, with 8.65 metres, only 1cm shy of the Greek national record.

At the World Indoor Championships at Glasgow in 2024, in March, he won a gold with a jump of 8.22 metres.

Serbiais fielding a team of 112 people, competing in 20 sports. The country’s biggest hope at the 2024 Olympics is its male basketball team, the current runner-up and, as of February, fourth on FIBA’s world ranking list.

Members of selections are NBA starts like three times NBA MVP [most valuable player] Nikola Jokic and well as Bogdan Bogdanovic and Vasilije Micic, as well as other highly successful players from Europe and domestic teams.

In five preparatory games, Serbia scored three wins. Team coach Svetislav Pesic said after the match on Tuesday that he is still concerned with the players’ physical condition.

“Players came with injuries that have not healed, so we had to do that in this training process; we had to improvise a bit, we had to really dedicate ourselves to the players,” Pesic told the media.

Another favourite for medals is the Serbian women’s volleyball team – holders of a bronze from the 2021 Olympics and a silver from 2016.

Team captain Maja Ognjenovic said ahead of leaving for Paris that the team “did all the preparations in a very good way, and after many years of long preparations these were not so long. We know that we are ready, both physically and mentally.

“We are waiting with great impatience to enter the Olympic village, but also to start the competition. You all know our goals and ambitions, and we hope to fulfil them,” Ognjenovic said.

Moldovais sending 26 athletes to the Paris Olympics who will compete in ten disciplines, two of which are new, table tennis and horse riding.

TV sports journalist Adrian Sau told BIRN that this year’s Moldovan team is stronger than ever, because many Moldovan athletes are going to Paris as European or world champions.

“Five athletes from Moldova have a good chance of winning medals at the Olympics – Anastasia Nichita in wrestling, Serghei Tarnavski in rowing, Irina Ringaci in wrestling, Denis Vieru in judo and Andrian Mardare in javelin throwing – plus the weightlifter Marin Robu,” Sau said.

Judoka Denis Vieru is world number one in his weight category, of 66 kilos. At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, rower Sergei Tarnovschi won a bronze medal.

Montenegrois pinning all its hopes on its men’s water polo team, which reached sixth place at the European Championship held this year in Croatia.

The “Sharks”, the nickname of the players, are led from the bench by one of the best players in the sport, Vladimir Gojkovic, who last played for the national team at the 2012 London Games.

“We are pleased that … the national team played at a very high level for the last ten days. We have time to fix some things,” Gojkovic told reporters after a recent match.

Wrestler Vladimir Egorov is one of only six athletes representingNorth Macedoniaat the Paris Olympics. This winner of several medals from major competitions is North Macedonian’s trump card for a medal in Paris.

“Like for every athlete, it is a career highlight for me to participate in the Olympics. In addition to all the results that I have behind me, I wish to enrich my collection with a medal from these Olympics,” Egorov said before heading for France.

Egorov’s first medal for North Macedonia was at the European Championship in Bucharest in 2019, where he won the bronze.

Bulgaria’s team in Paris numbers 108 members but most eyes are on 20-year-old weightlifter Karlos Nasar.

The Paris Olympics will be a comeback for him: he was born in Paris to a Bulgarian-Lebanese family but spent his childhood in the small Bulgarian town of Cherven bryag.

“There will be much attention on his performance as, at the end of June, Nasar broke three world records at the State Weightlifting Championship in Ruse,” sports journalist Bojidar Kartunov told BIRN.

Nasar himself feels optimistic. “People’s love is my fuel to be so motivated and strong. My fans count on me, and I’ll never betray them,” he told national television in May.

Romania’steam at the games in Paris comprises 107 athletes competing in 18 sports. In rowing, Romania has a record number of 45 athletes. Others are competing in swimming, cycling, boxing, polo, artistic gymnastics, fencing, tennis, table tennis, archery and yachting.

Sports journalist Emil Gradinescu told BIRN that Romania’s strong rowing delegation should win the most medals at these Olympics in Paris. “We have good chances in rowing. But we can expect a medal from David Popovici in swimming and maybe others in gymnastics, weightlifting and shooting,” Gradinescu said.

However, he said the management of the Romanian Olympic and Sports Committee could have been more successful. “Compared to other countries at our level, the Romanian team presents itself very poorly … With us in Romania, everyone is focused on football, and that’s it,” said Gradinescu.

Albania, which has never won an Olympic medal, will be represented in Paris by eight athletes. Runner Luiza Gega is the best known, having specialized in middle and long-distance running since 2016. Gega won a silver at the 2016 European Championships, and a gold in 2022.

title: Balkan Countries Eye Potential Medals at Paris Olympics | Balkan Insight
author: Hamdi Firat Buyuk, Vuk Tesija, Azem Kurtic, Eleni Stamatoukou, Milica Stojanovic, Borislav Visnjic, Sinisa Jakov Marusic, Svetoslav Todorov, Madalin Necsutu, Perparim Isufi, Fjori Sinoruka
description: As Balkan teams descend on Paris for the 2024 summer Olympics, their countries are pinning their highest hopes of success on those sports in which they have traditionally excelled.
